title: Chapter 32: The Story
content: The sand is cold.

Not California-postcard cold. Not the Mediterranean warmth I wrote about in Chapter 14, the one where Jax walked barefoot on Monaco gravel like she owned the coastline. This is real cold — Malibu at 5:47 AM, the tide pulling back like it's embarrassed to be seen, the sand stiff and damp under my palms as I dig.

Six inches. That's what I'm aiming for. Deep enough that a passing jogger won't kick it up. Shallow enough that I can find it again if I need to. My fingers ache from scraping — no shovel, no tools, just my hands and the darkness and the sound of the Pacific grinding itself against the shore like it has a complaint it can't articulate.

The cold wallet fits in the hole like it was measured for it. Small black rectangle. Innocent. The green LED on its face blinks once — a single pulse of digital life — before I cover it. I smooth the sand over, pat it down, scatter a handful of kelp across the surface. The beach looks the same as it did before I got here. The same beach where Jax stood for 714 nights, staring at encrypted horizons, waiting for signals that would never come back the way she sent them.
Now there's a signal under the sand. A green blink in a black wallet, pulsing to no one. A lighthouse with no ships.

I sit back on my heels and wipe my hands on my jeans. Sand sticks to my palms, gritty and mineral, and I can smell the salt cutting through the early morning fog. My fingers are trembling. Not from the cold.
From the choice I just made.

I had the coordinates. South Pacific — a point in the ocean so specific it had to be meaningful. I could have followed them. Bought a ticket, flown to Fiji, chartered a boat, found whatever Jax left at the end of that longitude and latitude. I could have walked straight into wherever she is and said: I found you.
I could have also walked straight into a trap. Or led the FBI straight to her. Or both.
So I buried it.

The green code blinks under six inches of Malibu sand, and I stand up and walk away from the water and don't look back, because looking back is what people do in stories when they want the audience to know something mattered. I already know it mattered. My sand-caked hands know. My heartbeat knows.

I drive home to Santa Monica with the windows down, letting the ocean air cut through the cabin of my car like a blade. The sunrise is just starting — pink and gold, gilded, the kind of light Jax would have called *performative*. She was right about that. Los Angeles sunrises are always showing off. They know they're being watched.

The apartment smells like instant ramen. It always smells like instant ramen now — the salty, dehydrated ghost of actual food, the taste of a life downsized. I boil water in the morning and the steam fogs the kitchen window and I eat standing up, scrolling through freelance gigs on my laptop, and I think: this is what surviving looks like.

It's not what living looked like. Living was rooftop parties and encrypted messages andJax's hand on the small of my back as she whispered blockchain addresses like love poems. Living was dangerous and electric and broke my heart into distributed fragments.
Surviving is ramen at 7 AM and a manuscript that's eating my life from the inside out.

Forty thousand words. That's where *Gilded Glitch* sits this morning, bloated and growing, a document I open every day that surprises me with its own weight. I write in the mornings — that's the rule I've given myself. Five hundred words before noon, no matter what. No editing, no second-guessing, just the raw feed of memory and imagination bleeding onto the page.

Then I close the laptop and become someone else. Afternoon Maya is a freelance video editor who colors corporate promos and trims talking-head interviews for companies that will never know she once deleted the single most-watched video in internet history. The irony is not lost on me. I sit in front of timelines all day, cutting and splicing other people's stories, and then I come home and write my own.

At night — 2 AM, usually, when the neighborhood hum has dropped to a whisper and the only light in my apartment is the pale glow of my screen — I read what I wrote that morning. And I wonder if any of it is true.

Not factually true. I know which parts happened and which parts I invented. But *true* in the way Jax used to talk about code being beautiful — not functional, but structurally, essentially right. I wonder if the version I'm writing is becoming more real than the version I lived.

Here's the mutation: I started this manuscript as documentation. A record. I was going to lay it all out — the parties, the money, the system Jax built, the way it collapsed — and let the reader decide what it meant. But somewhere around word 20,000, the document stopped being a mirror and started being a mold. I'm not reflecting reality anymore. I'm shaping it. Every sentence I write about Jax Vance becomes the definitive version of Jax Vance. Every scene I construct between us becomes the scene. The story isn't a record of what happened — it's becoming the only version that matters.
And maybe that's the point. Maybe that's the only kind of truth that survives.
Agent Reyes shows up on a Thursday.

I know her knock now — three sharp raps, evenly spaced, like she's timing them. Federal precision. I open the door and she's standing in my hallway looking different. Smaller, somehow. Or maybe just less armored. She's not wearing her badge on her hip today, and her coat is unbuttoned, and there's something almost — *almost* — respectful in the way she looks at me.
"Ms. Chen." She says it like she's tasting the name for the first time.
"Agent Reyes."
"Can I come in?"

I step aside. She walks in, surveys the apartment — the ramen-strewn kitchen, the bed unmade, the laptop open on the table with the manuscript glowing on its screen. She doesn't look at the screen. I notice that she doesn't look, which means she already knows what's on it.
"I know you're writing a book about Vance," she says.
I don't flinch. "It's fiction."
Reyes almost smiles. "The best fiction always is."
She sits down without being invited, choosing the chair by the window — the one with the view of the parking lot. She crosses her legs. She looks comfortable, and that makes me uncomfortable.
"I want to read it when you're done," she says.
"It's not done."
"I can wait."
"You're FBI. You don't wait. You subpoena."
"I'm not here to subpoena anything." She pauses. "I'm here to tell you something. Consider it professional courtesy."
The phrase *professional courtesy* from an FBI agent is a snake offering you a warm rock. I sit down across from her and wait.
"We traced the three hundred and forty million," Reyes says. "Shell company in Fiji."
My stomach drops. I keep my face perfectly still — I've had practice — but somewhere beneath my skin, every alarm I have is screaming.

"Company doesn't exist on paper," Reyes continues. "No incorporation documents. No registration. No tax ID. But it exists on the blockchain. We've been mapping the transaction lattice for six weeks. The money moved through fourteen wallets before it pooled." She leans forward. "We're getting closer, Ms. Chen."
She says it with quiet certainty. Not triumph — certainty. The kind of certainty that comes from watching a target drift into range.
"That's... interesting," I say.
"That's all you have to say?"
"I'm a video editor, Agent Reyes. I don't know anything about shell companies in Fiji."
She studies me for a long moment. Then she stands, buttons her coat, and moves toward the door.
"Write a good book, Ms. Chen. But don't write yourself into a corner."

She leaves. The door clicks shut. And I sit in the silence of my apartment with sand still under my fingernails and a cold wallet buried on a beach and a manuscript on my screen that just became a matter of federal interest.

The coordinates. The coordinates in the cold wallet are for Fiji — or near it. Somewhere in that same stretch of South Pacific water where Reyes's phantom shell company is pulsing on the blockchain. If Reyes is that close, if her lattice-mapping is narrowing, then the signal I buried this morning might not matter. The coordinates might lead exactly where she thinks they lead.
I need to warn Jax.
I have no way to contact Jax.

I stare at my phone like it's a door I can't open. Jax built invisible systems — routed messages through dead drops and blockchain echoes and protocols that don't exist on any network map. Reyes is building invisible cases — subpoenas and transaction lattices and shell company traces that look like nothing until they look like everything. Both of them are playing the long game. Patient. Methodical. Moving pieces I can't see on a board I don't understand.
And I'm the piece in the middle. The one that gets moved without knowing whose hand is reaching.
I make a decision.
It's not the safe decision. It's not the smart decision. But it's the only decision that feels like *mine*.

I open the manuscript. Chapter 18. The party at the Vance estate — the one where the champagne flowed like a waterfall and Jordan Keeler laughed too loud and Tom Hargrove's eyes tracked every exit like a man calculating risk. The one where Jax found me on the balcony and said: *The glitch isn't the error, Maya. The glitch is the art.*

I polish it. I tighten the prose and sharpen the dialogue and make sure every sensory detail — the briny sweetness of the oysters, the amber weight of the whiskey, the way the string lights made Jax's skin look gilded — is so precise that reading it feels like being there.
Then I publish it.
One chapter. A teaser. I upload it to a literary platform at 11:42 PM with no author bio and no publisher name and a title in caps: **GILDED GLITCH**.
I click *Publish*.

The click is small. Just a button. Just a pixel event on a screen. But it sounds like a gunshot in my quiet apartment. Or maybe that's just my heartbeat, which is suddenly doing something it hasn't done since Jax — racing toward a horizon I can't see.
I go to bed.
By morning, it has forty thousand reads.
"GILDED GLITCH: The True Story of Jax Vance."

That's the headline on a tech blog that picks it up first. Then another. Then a Substack. Then a Twitter thread with 200K impressions. Then a Discord server dedicated to decrypting the "fictional" blockchain addresses in the text, which are — I realize with a mix of pride and nausea — actual addresses, slightly modified, close enough to be recognizable to anyone who was there.
The comments erupt like a geyser:
*She's alive.*
*Faked her own death. Classic exit scam with a literary twist.*
*This is the most Gatsby thing ever. She's standing on the beach reaching for a green light.*
*Is this fiction or memoir? Because the shell company details are TOO specific.*
*The author is Maya Chen. Has to be. The video girl.*
*Vance is out there reading this and LAUGHING.*

I scroll through the comments with a cup of ramen cooling in my hands, the salty broth lingering on my tongue, and I watch the line between story and truth dissolve in real time. That's the point. That's the whole point. The line was always the point.

Because here's what Reyes can't subpoena: a novel. Here's what the blockchain can't trace: a narrative. Once *Gilded Glitch* is published as fiction, the truth becomes legally contested. Every fact I embedded in the prose can be waved away as invention. Every real address can be called an artistic choice. The manuscript isn't just a book — it's a fortress. A textual safe house where the truth hides in plain sight, protected by the armor of imagination.
Jax would love it. Jax would have *designed* it.

The thought is ice water. Did she? Did Jax — somewhere in her seven hundred and fourteen nights of standing on this beach, somewhere in her encrypted architectures and dead-drop protocols — did she plan this too? Did she know I would write it? Did she know the writing would become the defense?
I don't know. I can't know. And that not-knowing is the space where Jax has always lived — in the gap between what I understand and what she's already calculated.

That night, I lie in bed and stare at the ceiling and listen to Santa Monica breathe outside my window — distant traffic, a dog barking three blocks over, the muffled bass of someone's music through shared walls. The manuscript sits at 40,200 words on my laptop. Chapter 18 is live and multiplying across the internet like a virus with perfect prose.
My phone buzzes.

The vibration is small — just a phone on a nightstand, just a rectangle of glass and metal shifting an eighth of an inch against wood. But I feel it like a seismic event. My hand moves before my brain does, snatching the phone, squinting at the screen.

A text. From a number that doesn't exist — not in my contacts, not in any format I recognize. Ten digits that resolve to nothing when I stare at them, encoded somehow, routed through some architecture that Jax built in the spaces between networks.
The message is four sentences:
*You buried the wallet. Smart girl. But you should know: the coordinates were never for you. They were for Reyes.*
*Stay safe, M. — J*
I read it three times.
The first time, I feel relief — she's alive, she's out there, she's watching.
The second time, I feel fear — she's watching, she's always been watching, she knew I would bury it.
The third time, I feel something else. Something colder and sharper than either. Something that sits at the base of my skull like a chess piece clicking into position.
*The coordinates were never for you. They were for Reyes.*

The cold wallet in the Malibu sand. The South Pacific coordinates blinking green under six inches of beach. The shell company in Fiji that Reyes is so proud of tracing. All of it — *all of it* — was misdirection. Jax didn't leave the wallet for me to find her. She left it for me to *have*. Because she knew Reyes would get close. She knew the FBI would trace the blockchain lattice. She knew that eventually, the coordinates would be discovered — not by me, but by the case Reyes was building.

Jax wanted Reyes looking at the South Pacific. She wanted the FBI mapping transactions that lead to a dead end in Fiji. She wanted the entire weight of federal investigation pointed at the wrong ocean.
And she used me to do it.

Not maliciously. Not carelessly. Strategically. The way she does everything — with the precision of someone who sees the board ten moves ahead and plays each piece before the opponent even knows it's in play. I was a piece. I *am* a piece. The wallet was a move. The burial was a move. Even this text is a move — confirming what I already suspected, giving me just enough to understand the shape of the game without revealing her position.
She's not in the South Pacific.
She was never in the South Pacific.

She's somewhere else entirely, three steps ahead of everyone, running the long con from a place I can't imagine, and the only weapon she needs is the story I'm writing — because once it's published as fiction, the truth becomes a matter of interpretation, and interpretation is a maze with no center and no exit.

I put the phone down. The screen darkens. The room is quiet again, except for the Pacific — always the Pacific — grinding against the shore a few blocks away, erasing and erasing, the only force in Los Angeles that doesn't care about narratives.
I close my eyes.
Jax Vance is alive. She's playing. She's winning.
And the story I'm writing — the one I thought was mine — has always been hers.
